translation: Alif, Lam, Meem
commentary:
الٓمٓ

The Surah opens with the mysterious letters Alif Lam Meem. This is the third Surah in the Quran to begin with these letters, and it will be followed by Surah As-Sajdah. These opening letters establish a connection between the Surahs, inviting us to reflect on their shared themes and messages.

translation: These are verses of the wise Book
commentary:
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ

Those are the miraculous signs of the Book full of wisdom. The parallel with Surah Al-Baqarah is striking—there too we find "those are the signs of the Book." However, a key distinction emerges in the description. While Al-Baqarah was revealed in Madinah to a mixed audience at different spiritual levels, Surah Luqman was revealed in Makkah to believers who had already endured tremendous trials and reached a state of excellence in their faith.

The Quran is described as الْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ —the Wise Book. This wisdom permeates every aspect of the Surah, from its opening verses to its concluding lessons. The Book doesn't merely contain wisdom; it is itself characterized by wisdom in how it guides, teaches and elevates the human soul.

translation: As guidance and mercy for the doers of good
commentary:
هُدًى وَرَحْمَةً لِّلْمُحْسِنِينَ

Guidance and mercy for those who excel in their religion. Notice the specific audience: الْمُحْسِنِينَ —those who practice ihsan, excellence in faith. In Surah Al-Baqarah, the Quran was described as guidance for المتقين —people of taqwa. Here, we see an elevated station. The early believers in Makkah, having suffered through persecution and hardship, had already developed this excellence. For them, the Quran offers not just guidance but also رَحْمَةً —profound mercy and compassion.

This tells us something remarkable: as our spiritual state develops, the Quran reveals different dimensions of itself to us. The same verses that guide the beginner become sources of deep mercy and comfort for those who have reached ihsan.

translation: Who establish prayer and give zakah, and they, of the Hereafter, are certain [in faith]
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ

Those who establish prayer, give zakah, and have complete certainty in the Hereafter. The definition of the muhsineen begins with their actions: they don't merely perform prayer—they يُقِيمُونَ, they establish it, making it a firm pillar in their lives. They fulfill the obligation of zakah, demonstrating concern for society's wellbeing.

But notice the emphasis on their relationship with the Hereafter: وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ يُوقِنُونَ—"and they, especially regarding the Hereafter, they have certainty." The pronoun هُمْ ("they") is repeated for emphasis. Their conviction about the next life isn't mere belief—it's يَقِينٌ, absolute certainty that shapes every decision they make.

translation: Those are on [right] guidance from their Lord, and it is those who are the successful
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَلَىٰ هُدًى مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

Those are upon guidance from their Master, and those are the ones who will attain ultimate success. Allah ﷻ doesn't say they merely have guidance—they are عَلَىٰ هُدًى, firmly established upon guidance, like standing on solid ground. This guidance comes مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ—from their Rabb, their Master who nurtures and cares for them.

The repetition of أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ("those are") emphasizes both aspects of their state: they have guidance in this life, and they are ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ —the ultimately successful ones in the next. True success isn't measured by worldly achievements but by this divine guidance and its eternal consequences.

translation: And of the people is he who buys the amusement of speech to mislead [others] from the way of Allah without knowledge and who takes it in ridicule. Those will have a humiliating punishment
commentary:
وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يَشْتَرِى لَهْوَ ٱلْحَدِيثِ لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَيَتَّخِذَهَا هُزُوًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

Among people there is one who purchases entertaining talk to mislead from the path of Allah ﷻ without knowledge, and takes it as mockery. Those will have humiliating punishment.

The contrast is stark. After describing the believers who establish prayer and have certainty in the Hereafter, Allah ﷻ presents the opposite picture: وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ —among people there are those who invest in لَهْوَ ٱلْحَدِيثِ, entertaining speech or idle talk.

Historical context gives this profound meaning. When the Prophet ﷺ would recite the Quran to the Quraysh, they were captivated by its eloquence—after all, they prided themselves on their mastery of Arabic. The leadership recognized the danger: if people kept listening, they would embrace Islam. Their solution? One trader traveled to Syria and hired a singer to hold public concerts, deliberately scheduled when the Prophet ﷺ would be teaching, to distract people from the Quranic message.

But the ayah speaks to a timeless reality. The phrase يَشْتَرِى ("purchases") indicates investment—someone is putting resources into the entertainment industry. Today, massive industries exist solely to occupy people's free time: sports, movies, video games, social media. These aren't inherently evil, but the ayah warns us about their purpose: لِيُضِلَّ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ —to mislead from the path of Allah ﷻ.

Consider the profound irony: we discussed disposable income in previous passages, and now we confront disposable time. Just as people waste their wealth on meaningless pursuits, they waste their precious hours on entertainment that adds nothing to their knowledge or character. بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ—it contributes no knowledge, no growth, no benefit.

The entertainment industry doesn't merely waste time—it corrupts values. Things that should remain private and shameful become normalized through constant exposure. Vulgar language becomes commonplace. Inappropriate relationships are glamorized. Young people absorb these messages, wanting to dress, speak and behave like the entertainers they admire. A culture of misguidance spreads.

We live in an unprecedented era of immediate access to entertainment. You don't wait for a TV show to air—you can stream it instantly. If video isn't available, your music is ready. If you can't get a signal, thirty games are installed on your phone. Not a single moment of your day goes by without the option for entertainment. The question becomes: when do we have time for reflection, for connection with Allah ﷻ, for meaningful relationships?

وَيَتَّخِذَهَا هُزُوًا—and they take the path of Allah ﷻ as a joke. Those engaged in this industry might protest: "I'm not stopping anyone from practicing Islam. People have a choice." But Allah ﷻ says they don't even realize the misguidance they're creating. The casual dismissal of faith, the mockery of religious practice embedded in entertainment—these shape society's values in ways we don't immediately recognize.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ—those will have humiliating punishment. The punishment matches the crime. They humiliated the path of Allah ﷻ by treating it as insignificant; they will face humiliation in return.

translation: And when our verses are recited to him, he turns away arrogantly as if he had not heard them, as if there was in his ears deafness. So give him tidings of a painful punishment
commentary:
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا وَلَّىٰ مُسْتَكْبِرًا كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا كَأَنَّ فِىٓ أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْرًا ۖ فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

When Our signs are recited to him, he turns away in arrogance as though he did not hear them, as though there is heaviness in his ears. So give him news of painful punishment.

The description deepens. When Allah's ﷻ revelations are recited to such a person, وَلَّىٰ مُسْتَكْبِرًا—he turns away in arrogance. He doesn't engage, doesn't reflect, doesn't even give it consideration. كَأَن لَّمْ يَسْمَعْهَا—as though he hadn't even heard it.

The imagery becomes even more vivid: كَأَنَّ فِىٓ أُذُنَيْهِ وَقْرًا—as if both his ears have a barrier, a heaviness blocking them. It's as if he's wearing headphones, deliberately tuning out the message. This isn't about physical hearing—his spiritual ears are sealed by pride.

فَبَشِّرْهُ بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ—so give him "good news" of painful punishment. The irony is deliberate. بَشِّرْ usually means to give glad tidings, but here the "glad tidings" are of painful punishment—a severe warning wrapped in sarcasm for those who mock.

translation: Indeed, those who believe and do righteous deeds - for them are the Gardens of Pleasure
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلنَّعِيمِ

Indeed, those who believed and did righteous deeds—for them are Gardens of Bliss.

After the harsh warning, Allah ﷻ returns to encouragement. إِنَّ provides emphasis: indeed, certainly, those who believed and performed righteous deeds will have gardens—not just any gardens, but جَنَّـٰتُ ٱلنَّعِيمِ, Gardens of continuous blessings and comfort.

The structure parallels the earlier verses. Just as the muhsineen were described with their qualities and then their reward, now those who believe and act righteously receive their promise. The emphasis on عَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ (good deeds) reminds us that faith without action is incomplete. Belief must manifest in how we live.

translation: Wherein they abide eternally; [it is] the promise of Allah [which is] truth. And He is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

They will remain in them eternally. That is the promise of Allah ﷻ in truth, and He is the Almighty, the All-Wise.

خَـٰلِدِينَ—remaining forever. This isn't temporary happiness or fleeting pleasure. The gardens are eternal, and so is the joy within them.

وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقًّا—this is Allah's ﷻ promise, and it is حَقًّا, absolute truth, reality, certain to be fulfilled. When Allah ﷻ promises something, there is no doubt about its occurrence.

وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ—and He is Al-Azeez, Al-Hakeem. He is the Almighty—no one can prevent Him from fulfilling His promise. He is the All-Wise—His promise is given with perfect wisdom, at the perfect time, in the perfect way. These names aren't arbitrary; they reinforce why His promise is absolutely trustworthy. His might ensures He can deliver it; His wisdom ensures He will deliver it perfectly.

translation: He created the heavens without pillars that you see and has cast into the earth firmly set mountains, lest it should shift with you, and dispersed therein from every creature. And We sent down rain from the sky and made grow therein [plants] of every noble kind
commentary:
خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا ۖ وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ

He created the heavens without pillars that you see, and placed firm mountains upon the earth lest it shake with you, and dispersed throughout it every kind of creature. And We sent down water from the sky and caused every noble pair to grow in it.

Having established the reality of the Hereafter and the promise of gardens, Allah ﷻ now directs our attention to the visible signs of His creative power. خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ بِغَيْرِ عَمَدٍ تَرَوْنَهَا—He created the heavens without pillars that you can see. When we construct buildings, we first see the support beams, the pillars, the framework. Only then do we see the finished product. But Allah ﷻ created the vast expanse above us without any visible means of support. The heavens simply exist, held in place by His command alone.

The word السَّمَـٰوَٰتِ encompasses not just the sky but everything above us—the atmosphere, space, the celestial bodies. It's all there without any pillar you can observe holding it up.

وَأَلْقَىٰ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ—and He cast upon the earth firm mountains. The verb أَلْقَىٰ suggests something dropped or thrown down, mountains placed upon the earth like anchors. أَن تَمِيدَ بِكُمْ—lest it shake with you. The mountains serve as stabilizers, preventing the earth from moving erratically beneath our feet. The word تَمِيدَ comes from ميد, which means to sway, to slip, to move unsteadily.

These mountains aren't just beautiful scenery—they serve a crucial function in the earth's stability. Modern geology has discovered that mountains have deep roots extending into the earth's crust, acting exactly as the Quran describes: as stabilizers preventing excessive tectonic movement.

وَبَثَّ فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ دَآبَّةٍ—and He dispersed throughout it every kind of creature. The verb بَثَّ means to spread out, to distribute. Allah ﷻ didn't concentrate all life in one place; He spread it across the earth, filling every suitable environment with دَآبَّةٍ—creatures that move upon it.

وَأَنزَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً—and We sent down water from the sky. Notice the shift from third person (He created) to first person plural (We sent down). This is the royal "We," emphasizing Allah's ﷻ majesty and power in this act of providing sustenance.

فَأَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ—then We caused to grow from it every noble pair. The word زَوْجٍ indicates pairs, variety, different types. And each type is described as كَرِيمٍ—noble, generous, gracious. The plants that grow aren't just functional; they possess beauty, dignity, benefit.

There's a profound message here about entertainment versus observation. People obsessed with manufactured entertainment—screens, games, constant stimulation—have no time to observe the magnificulous beauty of creation around them. They're too absorbed in artificial worlds to appreciate the real one. We can live in places of stunning natural beauty—forests, mountains, coastlines—and never truly see them because our eyes are fixed on a device.

Allah ﷻ is calling us to look up, look around, go outside. See the water falling from the sky. Watch the plants growing. Observe the mountains standing firm. This is real entertainment, real education, real connection with the Creator through His creation.

translation: This is the creation of Allah. So show Me what those other than Him have created. Rather, the wrongdoers are in clear error
commentary:
هَـٰذَا خَلْقُ ٱللَّهِ فَأَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦ ۚ بَلِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

This is the creation of Allah ﷻ, so show Me what those besides Him have created. Rather, the wrongdoers are in clear error.

After displaying the signs of His creation, Allah ﷻ issues a challenge: هَـٰذَا خَلْقُ ٱللَّهِ—this is Allah's ﷻ creation. It's right in front of you, undeniable, magnificent. فَأَرُونِى مَاذَا خَلَقَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن دُونِهِۦ—now show Me what those you worship besides Him have created. What have your idols made? What have your false gods brought into existence?

The challenge is rhetorical, of course. The answer is obvious: nothing. They haven't created anything. They are themselves created objects, powerless and lifeless.

بَلِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ—rather, the wrongdoers are in clear misguidance. The word ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (the wrongdoers) is significant. These aren't just misguided people; they are wrongdoers. Why? Because associating partners with Allah ﷻ is the ultimate wrong, as we'll see explicitly stated in the coming verses. They wrong themselves by this association, and they are فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ—in clear, obvious, undeniable error.

translation: And We had certainly given Luqman wisdom [and said], "Be grateful to Allah." And whoever is grateful is grateful for [the benefit of] himself. And whoever denies [His favor] - then indeed, Allah is Free of need and Praiseworthy
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا لُقْمَـٰنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ ۚ وَمَن يَشْكُرْ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ

We had certainly given Luqman wisdom: "Be grateful to Allah ﷻ." And whoever is grateful is only grateful for himself. And whoever is ungrateful—then indeed, Allah ﷻ is Free of need, Praiseworthy.

Now we enter a remarkable passage about a wise man named Luqman. He wasn't a prophet—there's no reliable evidence establishing prophethood for him—but he was someone blessed with wisdom, known among the Arabs even before Islam for his insight and sound counsel.

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا لُقْمَـٰنَ ٱلْحِكْمَةَ—We had certainly given Luqman wisdom. Notice that Allah ﷻ attributes the wisdom to Himself: We gave it to him. The ayah doesn't say "Luqman was wise." This teaches us immediately that wisdom is a gift from Allah ﷻ, not a personal achievement to boast about. Even Luqman's wisdom was granted by Allah ﷻ.

أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ—that he should be grateful to Allah ﷻ. The أَنِ here indicates purpose or content: the wisdom was given with this message, or the essence of the wisdom was this. Be grateful to Allah ﷻ. Notice it's in command form: ٱشْكُرْ. Commands in Arabic convey urgency, immediate necessity. This isn't a suggestion or recommendation—it's an urgent matter requiring immediate attention.

So the height of wisdom is recognizing that gratitude to Allah ﷻ must be your priority. Everything else flows from this. The wisest person is someone who sees clearly that they must be grateful to Allah ﷻ. This connects back to how the Surah opened, describing the Quran as الْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ—the Book full of wisdom. Where does that wise book begin? الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ—All praise and gratitude belongs to Allah ﷻ, Lord of all the worlds. The Quran's wisdom begins with gratitude.

Consider also the example of Prophet Ibrahim (AS), whose wisdom, genius, foresight and communication skills we admire. How does Allah ﷻ summarize his character? شَاكِرًا لِّأَنْعُمِهِ—he was grateful for His blessings. The defining characteristic of this wise prophet was gratitude.

Understanding this connection helps us appreciate why gratitude and humility are inseparable, two sides of the same coin. When you're grateful, you're giving credit to someone else. You're acknowledging that what you have comes from beyond yourself. This naturally produces humility. Conversely, when you give credit to yourself—to your intelligence, your hard work, your abilities—you become full of pride. You can't be truly grateful and arrogant simultaneously. It's impossible.

This will become important as we continue through Luqman's advice to his son. He'll teach him not to be arrogant, which is the necessary consequence of being grateful. Gratitude produces humility; ingratitude produces pride.

وَمَن يَشْكُرْ فَإِنَّمَا يَشْكُرُ لِنَفْسِهِۦ—and whoever is grateful is only grateful for himself. The benefit of gratitude returns to you. When you thank Allah ﷻ, you're not doing Him a favor. He doesn't need your thanks. Rather, gratitude transforms you. It changes your perspective, opens your heart, increases your blessings, protects you from arrogance, and connects you more deeply to your Creator. You are the beneficiary.

Notice something crucial in the Arabic: أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ—be grateful to Allah ﷻ. The لِ here means "to" or "for." It doesn't say اشْكُرِ اللَّهَ only, which would mean thank only Allah ﷻ and no one else. By saying be grateful "to Allah ﷻ," it includes being grateful to Him while also being grateful to everyone else who deserves thanks—parents, teachers, friends, anyone who has done good for you. Gratitude to Allah ﷻ doesn't negate gratitude to His creation; rather, it encompasses and elevates it. When you thank your parents, you're ultimately thanking Allah ﷻ who sent them to you as a mercy.

وَمَن كَفَرَ—and whoever is ungrateful. The word كَفَرَ is powerful. It's in past tense: whoever showed ingratitude even once, whoever covered over Allah's ﷻ blessings even briefly. This captures how serious ingratitude is.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ حَمِيدٌ—then indeed Allah ﷻ is Ghaniyy, Hameed. He is غَنِىٌّ—completely free of need, absolutely independent and self-sufficient. He doesn't need your worship, your gratitude, your recognition. But wealth alone doesn't make someone praiseworthy. Plenty of wealthy people are terrible. So Allah ﷻ adds: He is حَمِيدٌ—inherently praiseworthy.

The difference between Hameed and Mahmoud is subtle but meaningful. Mahmoud means "praised by someone"—there's an implied "by" in the word. Someone is doing the praising. But Hameed means praised in and of Himself, without needing anyone to praise Him. Whether you praise Allah ﷻ or not, whether you exist or not, He is Hameed. His praiseworthiness is intrinsic, independent, eternal. He doesn't become praiseworthy because we praise Him. He is praiseworthy, period.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when Luqman said to his son while he was instructing him, "O my son, do not associate [anything] with Allah. Indeed, association [with him] is great injustice
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَـٰنُ لِٱبْنِهِۦ وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُۥ يَـٰبُنَىَّ لَا تُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱلشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ

And when Luqman said to his son while he was advising him, "O my son, do not associate anything with Allah ﷻ. Indeed, shirk is a tremendous wrong."

وَإِذْ قَالَ لُقْمَـٰنُ لِٱبْنِهِۦ—and remember when Luqman said to his son. The إِذْ means "when" but also implies "remember that moment, don't forget it." This wasn't a casual conversation—it was a defining moment of counsel worth preserving and remembering.

وَهُوَ يَعِظُهُۥ—while he was advising him. The word يَعِظُهُۥ indicates counsel, admonishment, meaningful advice. The father chose his moment carefully. His son wasn't busy, wasn't distracted, wasn't in the middle of something else. Perhaps they were sitting together, the son had come seeking advice about some matter, and the father seized this opportunity when his son's heart was open and receptive.

The father-son dynamic is crucial to understand in context. Earlier in the Surah, Allah ﷻ mentioned the entertainment industry and its power to mislead—particularly appealing to youth. Now we see the antidote: a strong father who engages with his son, who takes time to counsel him, who recognizes the moments when his words will penetrate the heart.

يَـٰبُنَىَّ—O my dear son. The term بُنَىَّ is deeply affectionate, like "my little boy" or "my dear young one." It's the same tender expression Prophet Yaqub (AS) used with his son: يَا بُنَيَّ. The father isn't being cold or distant. He puts his arm around his son, showing love and warmth. This is counsel wrapped in affection.

لَا تُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ—do not associate anything with Allah ﷻ. Imagine the scene: father and son talking, perhaps about everyday matters—career choices, marriage, business decisions. The son is asking advice about practical things. And right in the middle of this counsel about life, the father turns and says, "Son, don't do shirk with Allah ﷻ."

It might seem like an abrupt change of subject. But understand what the father is seeing. Maybe his son is getting confident, successful. "Dad, I got picked for the team. I have five job offers, one is even six figures." The father responds, "Don't do shirk with Allah ﷻ." Where did all those opportunities come from? From Allah ﷻ. The moment you start attributing them to your abilities alone, the moment you start appreciating yourself instead of appreciating Allah ﷻ, you're heading toward shirk.

Or perhaps the son comes worried: "Dad, the economy is terrible. I can't find a job. I don't know what's going to happen." The father says, "Son, don't do shirk with Allah ﷻ." Don't place your reliance on the economy, on the job market, on your degree. Place it on Allah ﷻ. He is the Provider, not these worldly means.

This is the subtle, internal dimension of shirk—not worshipping idols, but putting your trust, your hope, your sense of security in things other than Allah ﷻ. Notice the connection to gratitude: شُكْر (gratitude) and شِرْك share the same root letters, just in different sequence. Shirk is the ultimate ingratitude—giving credit to the wrong party, associating others with Allah ﷻ in what belongs to Him alone.

إِنَّ ٱلشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ—indeed, shirk is a tremendous wrong. The word ظُلْمٌ means wrong, injustice, putting something in the wrong place. And it's عَظِيمٌ—tremendous, enormous, grave. You will make no greater mistake than this. The father isn't being dramatic. He's teaching his son the reality: if you get everything else right but get this wrong, you've lost everything. And if you get this right, everything else becomes manageable.

This is why, after establishing that wisdom means being grateful to Allah ﷻ, the very first piece of specific advice is: don't do shirk. Don't fall into ingratitude of the highest order. Don't associate partners with Allah ﷻ. Keep your gratitude, your trust, your worship directed purely to Him.

translation: And We have enjoined upon man [care] for his parents. His mother carried him, [increasing her] in weakness upon weakness, and his weaning is in two years. Be grateful to Me and to your parents; to Me is the [final] destination
commentary:
وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ وَهْنًا عَلَىٰ وَهْنٍ وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ فِى عَامَيْنِ أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَيْكَ إِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

And We have commanded mankind concerning his parents. His mother carried him in weakness upon weakness, and his weaning takes two years. Be grateful to Me and to your parents; to Me is the final return.

The transition here is beautiful. Luqman was advising his son, teaching him gratitude and warning him against shirk. Now Allah ﷻ directly addresses the question of parents' rights. Why this shift?

Because we might listen to Luqman's advice and think, "What a wonderful father, teaching his son about faith!" But then a parent might ask, "What about our rights? What about the son's obligation to the father? Where's the passage about Luqman's son being good to him?"

Instead of narrating what Luqman's son should say to him, Allah ﷻ Himself speaks up to defend parents' rights. It's as if Allah ﷻ is saying, "Let Me handle this part."

وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ—We have enjoined upon mankind concerning both parents. The word وَصَّيْنَا indicates a strong command, an obligation, an emphasis. And it begins with both parents: بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ. The father was already mentioned in the previous ayah—there was Luqman counseling his son. So we know the father's great contribution: he teaches, advises, serves as a role model. But what about the mother?

حَمَلَتْهُ أُمُّهُۥ وَهْنًا عَلَىٰ وَهْنٍ—his mother carried him in weakness upon weakness. The word وَهْنًا comes from وَهَن, meaning weakness, frailty. She carried him while experiencing weakness upon weakness. The pregnancy becomes harder and harder. She starts feeling nauseous, can't eat certain foods, can't sleep comfortably at night. Her back aches increasingly. She gets cramps in her legs. The baby draws nutrition from whatever she eats, leaving her constantly hungry and weak. Then the culmination—she nearly dies delivering him.

وَفِصَـٰلُهُۥ فِى عَامَيْنِ—and his weaning is in two years. After the ordeal of pregnancy and delivery, she breastfeeds him for up to two years. This is the mother's great, irreplaceable contribution.

Here's the profound difference: the mother's favors are natural, automatic. She doesn't need to take a course in pregnancy. It just happens. She carries, delivers, and nurses. These monumental contributions occur whether she's Muslim or not, religious or not, wise or not. Every mother makes this contribution simply by being a mother.

But the father's contribution requires wisdom. He must choose to be present, to counsel wisely, to be a good role model. He must be granted wisdom like Luqman. Not every father automatically becomes a positive contributor. He has to work for it, pray for it, develop it.

This is why the mother's favors are emphasized by Allah ﷻ—because they happened when the child can't even remember. The father's favors are easier to recall because they come later: "Dad, can I have five dollars? Can we go to the park? Can you teach me this? Can I come with you?" These are conscious memories.

But the mother's greatest sacrifices occurred before memory began. So Allah ﷻ reminds us, ensuring we don't forget what we cannot naturally remember.

أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِى وَلِوَٰلِدَيْكَ—be grateful to Me and to your parents. Look at how the ayah began and how it ends: وَوَصَّيْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ بِوَٰلِدَيْهِ—it began with both parents. Now it concludes: وَلِوَٰلِدَيْكَ—and to both your parents. But in between, only the mother is mentioned in detail because she's the one whose favors might be forgotten. The father's role was already implied in the previous passage about Luqman.

إِلَىَّ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—to Me is the final return. You will answer to Me for how you treated them. The greatest treasure you have is your iman, your faith. Who gave you that? Often, it's your parents—particularly a father like Luqman who taught you about Allah ﷻ. So gratitude to Allah ﷻ naturally extends to gratitude to those who were the means of your guidance.

translation: But if they endeavor to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, do not obey them but accompany them in [this] world with appropriate kindness and follow the way of those who turn back to Me [in repentance]. Then to Me will be your return, and I will inform you about what you used to do
commentary:
وَإِن جَـٰهَدَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُشْرِكَ بِى مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا ۖ وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا ۖ وَٱتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَىَّ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ

But if they struggle with you to make you associate with Me that of which you have no knowledge, then do not obey them. But accompany them in this world with kindness, and follow the path of those who turn back to Me. Then to Me is your return, and I will inform you of what you used to do.

This ayah addresses a difficult scenario, one that mirrors ayah 29 of Surah Al-Ankabut with an important addition. Luqman told his son not to do shirk, but what if parents are commanding shirk?

وَإِن جَـٰهَدَاكَ عَلَىٰٓ أَن تُشْرِكَ بِى—and if they struggle with you to make you associate partners with Me. The verb جَـٰهَدَاكَ is strong—it means to strive hard, to struggle intensely. They're not just suggesting or requesting. They're putting pressure on you, making it difficult, perhaps even threatening consequences if you don't comply.

مَا لَيْسَ لَكَ بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ—that which you have no knowledge of. You don't know this other deity they want you to worship. You have no evidence for it, no understanding of why it deserves worship. It's false, baseless.

فَلَا تُطِعْهُمَا—then do not obey them. The command is clear. When obedience to parents conflicts with obedience to Allah ﷻ, you must choose Allah ﷻ. There is no obedience to the creation in disobedience to the Creator. This principle is absolute.

But immediately, before you might think this means cutting off your parents, Allah ﷻ adds: وَصَاحِبْهُمَا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا مَعْرُوفًا—but accompany them in this world with kindness.

صَاحِبْهُمَا means to keep company with them, to maintain close, constant contact. Even if every time you visit them they tell you to worship their idols, to abandon Islam, to do shirk—you still visit. You still call every day. You still spend time with them. You can't abandon them. Don't obey their command to do shirk, but continue treating them with excellence.

مَعْرُوفًا—in what is known to be right, in the recognized good way. The word مَعْرُوفًا is significant. It refers to what is already known, already recognized as good. You don't need the Quran to tell you that you should care for your parents. You already knew your mother carried you in pain, delivered you, fed you for years. You already knew your father worked to provide for you, taught you, protected you. These facts were مَعْرُوفًا—already known to you before Islam. The Quran is simply reminding you, not revealing new information.

وَٱتَّبِعْ سَبِيلَ مَنْ أَنَابَ إِلَىَّ—and follow the path of those who turn back to Me. Here's the crucial balance: you keep company with your parents regardless of their faith, but you follow the lifestyle of those who have turned to Allah ﷻ. Your social time, your companionship belongs to your parents. But your way of life, your values, your practices—these you take from the righteous, from those who have repented and returned to Allah ﷻ.

This echoes the end of Surah Al-Ankabut: those who struggle for Our sake, We guide them to Our paths, and We enter them into the company of the righteous. You find righteous friends, righteous community, righteous role models—people who have turned back to Allah ﷻ—and you follow their path.

ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ—then to Me is your place of return. All of you—you and your parents—will return to Allah . فَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ—and I will inform you of what you used to do. You'll be held accountable for your deeds, they'll be held accountable for theirs. You cannot control their choices, but you can control yours. Treat them well, refuse to disobey Allah ﷻ, and leave the judgment to Him.

translation: [And Luqman said], "O my son, indeed if wrong should be the weight of a mustard seed and should be within a rock or [anywhere] in the heavens or in the earth, Allah will bring it forth. Indeed, Allah is Subtle and Acquainted
commentary:
يَـٰبُنَىَّ إِنَّهَآ إِن تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ فَتَكُن فِى صَخْرَةٍ أَوْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ أَوْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَأْتِ بِهَا ٱللَّهُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ

O my son, indeed if it should be the weight of a mustard seed and should be within a rock or in the heavens or in the earth, Allah ﷻ will bring it forth. Indeed, Allah ﷻ is Subtle, All-Aware.

Now we return to Luqman's direct counsel to his son, and this advice is remarkable in its imagery.

يَـٰبُنَىَّ—O my beloved son. Again, that tender address. إِنَّهَآ إِن تَكُ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ—indeed, if there should be even the weight of a mustard seed. A mustard seed is tiny, almost invisible. مِثْقَالَ means the weight of, the measure of. Even if we're talking about something as small as a mustard seed's weight.

فَتَكُن فِى صَخْرَةٍ—and it happens to be inside a rock, a boulder. Imagine: there's a tiny mustard seed, and it's deep inside a massive boulder.

أَوْ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ أَوْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—or anywhere in the heavens or in the earth. So this boulder containing the seed could be anywhere in the entire universe. It could be on Mars, in another galaxy, at the bottom of an ocean, inside a mountain range, buried in a desert—anywhere at all in all of creation.

Let's think about the impossibility of finding this seed. First, you'd have to search every boulder in the entire universe. Then, even when you found the right boulder, you'd have to break it open. But if you just crack it in half, the seed might still be embedded invisibly in either half. You'd have to literally pulverize that boulder into pieces the size of mustard seeds to find it. The task is impossible for any created being.

يَأْتِ بِهَا ٱللَّهُ—Allah ﷻ will bring it forth. That well-guarded, impossible-to-reach secret, Allah ﷻ will bring it out and say, "Here it is."

What is Luqman teaching his son? Nothing is hidden from Allah ﷻ. Not your actions, not your intentions, not your thoughts, not your secret deeds. That tiny seed in an unknown boulder in an unknown location in the vastness of the universe—Allah ﷻ knows exactly where it is, and He can bring it forth at will.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَطِيفٌ خَبِيرٌ—indeed, Allah ﷻ is Lateef, Khabeer.

لَطِيفٌ is a profound name, difficult to capture in translation. It implies subtlety, precision, gentleness. Imagine telling a very strong person—someone muscular who doesn't know their own strength—to hold a straw without breaking it. They'd crush it immediately. Too much strength, no control, no finesse.

But to extract that seed from the boulder requires both immense power and perfect precision. Allah ﷻ has the power to break through any barrier, yet He also has the subtlety to do it with perfect precision, without disturbing anything else. He is Lateef—subtle and precise in His actions.

The word also relates to Allah's ﷻ knowledge of subtle things. In another ayah, we learn that He knows خَائِنَةَ الْأَعْيُنِ—the treachery of the eyes. You're walking down the street and you see something you shouldn't look at. You try to keep your eyes lowered, but your pupil moves just slightly, stealing a glance that no one else notices. Allah ﷻ captured that. He recorded that tiny, subtle movement of your eye. That's His subtlety—nothing escapes Him, no matter how small or hidden.

خَبِيرٌ —All-Aware. He has complete, thorough awareness of everything. Not just surface knowledge, but deep, comprehensive understanding of all things, seen and unseen, evident and hidden.

The message to the son is powerful: You're about to be accountable. You now know Allah ﷻ sees everything. Better establish prayer and live accordingly.

translation: O my son, establish prayer, enjoin what is right, forbid what is wrong, and be patient over what befalls you. Indeed, [all] that is of the matters [requiring] determination
commentary:
يَـٰبُنَىَّ أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَٱنْهَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَكَ ۖ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ

O my son, establish prayer, command what is right, forbid what is wrong, and be patient over what befalls you. Indeed, that is of the matters requiring resolve.

After establishing that Allah ﷻ sees everything and will bring every hidden deed to account, Luqman moves to the practical application.

يَـٰبُنَىَّ أَقِمِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ—O my son, establish the prayer. Not just pray occasionally or mechanically, but أَقِمِ—establish it, make it firm, make it a central pillar of your life. Prayer is the practical manifestation of gratitude to Allah ﷻ. It's where you physically demonstrate your submission, your humility, your recognition of His greatness.

وَأْمُرْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ—and command what is right. Our religion is built on justice. Prayer instills this sense of justice in you. When you establish salah, it puts something inside you—a consciousness that Allah ﷻ is just, that He expects justice from you, and that the world around you should be fair.

So you stand up for what's right. You encourage good. Your coworkers are making fun of an employee behind his back—you say, "This isn't right. We shouldn't do this." Your manager wants to overbill a client—you say, "I'm not comfortable with that. It's not ethical." You see kids vandalizing property—you tell them to stop, even though they might give you trouble for it.

This is أْمُرْ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ—standing upright morally, calling others to decency, encouraging what's good and honorable.

وَٱنْهَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ—and forbid what is wrong. Speak out against evil. When you see something wrong happening, don't stay silent. Say something. You don't have to quote Quran or hadith. Just say, "This is wrong. We can't do this."

Prayer gives you the courage to do this. When you stand before Allah ﷻ five times a day, you realize you don't need protection from anyone except Allah ﷻ. You become brave because you're in the company of the Almighty. No human can harm you without His permission, so why should you fear speaking the truth?

وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَكَ—and be patient over what befalls you. When you stand up for truth, trouble will come. People will oppose you. They might mock you, threaten you, make your life difficult. Be mentally prepared for it. Have patience.

This connects to the earlier pairing: gratitude and patience are two sides of one coin, two parts of a believer's personality. The scholar Ibn Qayyim said that a believer's life is defined by الشُّكْرُ وَالصَّبْرُ—gratitude and patience. Luqman started his advice with gratitude (be grateful to Allah ﷻ), and now he ends this section with patience.

These two qualities support each other. When you're having a hard time, you remain patient by remembering what you have to be grateful for. Focus on your blessings, and it gives you strength to endure the trial. Conversely, if you're impatient, you lose sight of gratitude. You open the fridge, see there's no Pepsi—only Diet Coke, Gatorade, milk—and you complain about the missing Pepsi. You found five reasons to be grateful but fixated on the one reason to be upset. Impatience breeds ingratitude; patience preserves gratitude.

إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ—indeed, that is of the matters requiring resolve. The word عَزْمِ comes from عَزْم, meaning determination, firm resolve, decisiveness. These aren't easy commands. Establishing prayer consistently, standing up for truth, forbidding evil, remaining patient through hardship—these require real strength of character. They're among the noblest, most demanding accomplishments you can achieve. But they're also the most rewarding.

translation: And do not turn your cheek [in contempt] toward people and do not walk through the earth exultantly. Indeed, Allah does not like everyone self-deluded and boastful
commentary:
وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ

And do not turn your cheek away from people, and do not walk upon the earth arrogantly. Indeed, Allah ﷻ does not like every self-deluded boaster.

Having addressed gratitude and its natural companion humility, Luqman now teaches his son about the outward expressions of these inner qualities.

وَلَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ—and do not turn your cheek away from people. The word تُصَعِّرْ comes from a root meaning to swell or puff up one's cheek. It refers to the facial expression of pride or disdain—that look of arrogance or contempt people make when they want to show superiority.

We all know this face. We've made it, and we recognize when others make it. It's the dismissive look, the contemptuous glance, the expression that says, "I'm better than you." Luqman isn't just telling his son not to say mean things or do bad things to people. He's saying: don't even make that face.

Parents tell their children, "Why are you giving me that look?" The child protests, "I didn't say anything!" But the face said everything. The Quran acknowledges this reality—even your facial expressions must reflect humility and respect.

وَلَا تَمْشِ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مَرَحًا—and do not walk upon the earth in arrogance. The word مَرَحًا implies walking boastfully, strutting, showing off. Don't walk around posing, pretending to be something you're not, puffing yourself up.

Young men are especially prone to this—working out for a few months, then walking around in sleeveless shirts, flexing, demanding attention and admiration. This is the behavior Luqman warns against.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ كُلَّ مُخْتَالٍ فَخُورٍ—indeed, Allah ﷻ does not like every self-deluded boaster. The word مُخْتَالٍ is fascinating. It comes from خَيَال, meaning imagination or fantasy. A beautiful horse that struts is called a خَيَال because it walks as if it's performing, as if it's imagining everyone's admiration.

When applied to a person, مُخْتَالٍ means someone who has an imaginary perception of how amazing they are. They're full of themselves, strutting around based on a fantasy of their own greatness. They've constructed a virtual self in their mind, and they're presenting that false image to the world.

فَخُورٍ means extremely full of pride. This represents stages of arrogance: first comes مَرَحًا (showing off), then مُخْتَالٍ (becoming deluded about your greatness), then فَخُورٍ (becoming extremely boastful). It's a progression, one level of pride leading to the next, leading to the next.

Allah ﷻ doesn't love such people. His displeasure with arrogance is clear and emphatic.

translation: And be moderate in your pace and lower your voice; indeed, the most disagreeable of sounds is the voice of donkeys
commentary:
وَٱقْصِدْ فِى مَشْيِكَ وَٱغْضُضْ مِن صَوْتِكَ ۚ إِنَّ أَنكَرَ ٱلْأَصْوَٰتِ لَصَوْتُ ٱلْحَمِيرِ

Be moderate in your pace and lower your voice. Indeed, the most disagreeable of sounds is the voice of donkeys.

After addressing facial expressions and manner of walking, Luqman completes the lesson on humility's outward manifestations.

وَٱقْصِدْ فِى مَشْيِكَ—and be moderate in your pace. The word ٱقْصِدْ means to aim for the middle, to be balanced, to avoid extremes. Walk normally. Don't drag yourself along with your head down and shoulders slumped as if you're depressed—that's not the humility Islam calls for. But also don't strut around like you're in a show. Just walk with dignity and balance.

Earlier, Allah ﷻ described the servants of Ar-Rahman: وَعِبَادُ الرَّحْمَٰنِ الَّذِينَ يَمْشُونَ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ هَوْنًا—the servants of the Most Merciful are those who walk upon the earth humbly, with dignity. That's the balance: self-respect without arrogance, humility without false meekness.

وَٱغْضُضْ مِن صَوْتِكَ—and lower your voice. Now we move from body language to voice. The command is especially relevant when you're in a heated situation. You're trying to command good and forbid evil—which Luqman just told you to do—and someone gets upset with you, starts arguing, raises their voice at you.

Your first instinct might be to raise your voice back, to match their energy. But Luqman says: No. Lower your voice. Be patient—and patience means controlling your tone. It means not making faces at the person yelling at you. It means walking away with dignity, not stomping off angrily. It means keeping your voice low no matter how ignorant or aggressive the other person becomes.

This is real patience—not just enduring the trial, but maintaining your character throughout it.

إِنَّ أَنكَرَ ٱلْأَصْوَٰتِ لَصَوْتُ ٱلْحَمِيرِ—indeed, the most disagreeable of sounds is the voice of donkeys. The comparison is striking. Donkeys in most cultures symbolize stupidity. By comparing his son's potential raised voice to a donkey's braying, Luqman is making a powerful point.

Remember, this entire passage began with Luqman being given wisdom. The height of wisdom is gratitude to Allah ﷻ. But if you've received all this knowledge—how not to do shirk, how to be patient, how to command good—and then you lose control and raise your voice, it means all that knowledge didn't penetrate your heart. It's just sitting on your back like books on a donkey.

The Quran elsewhere uses this exact imagery: مَثَلُ الَّذِينَ حُمِّلُوا التَّوْرَاةَ ثُمَّ لَمْ يَحْمِلُوهَا كَمَثَلِ الْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًا—the example of those who were entrusted with the Torah and then did not uphold it is like that of a donkey who carries volumes of books. You have the knowledge, but you don't benefit from it. It's external, not internalized.

So Luqman is telling his son: Don't take all your knowledge and waste it. Don't be like a donkey carrying books it can't read. Internalize this wisdom. Let it change how you speak, how you walk, how you express yourself.

translation: Do you not see that Allah has made subject to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth and amply bestowed upon you His favors, [both] apparent and unapparent? But of the people is he who disputes about Allah without knowledge or guidance or an enlightening Book [from Him]
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُۥ ظَـٰهِرَةً وَبَاطِنَةً ۗ وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلَا هُدًى وَلَا كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ

Do you not see that Allah ﷻ has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, and has completed His favors upon you, apparent and hidden? Yet among people there are those who argue about Allah ﷻ without knowledge, guidance, or an enlightening book.

The Surah's opening section concludes with a return to the dual themes that have run throughout: humility and gratitude.

أَلَمْ تَرَوْا۟ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَخَّرَ لَكُم مَّا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—do you not see that Allah ﷻ has subjected to you whatever is in the heavens and earth? The word سَخَّرَ means to subdue, to humble, to make something submit. Everything in creation has been made subservient to Allah ﷻ and, by His grace, placed in service of humanity.

When you look at the universe operating in perfect order—the sun following its course without deviation, the moon maintaining its phases, the stars holding their positions—you're witnessing humility. The entire cosmos demonstrates submission to Allah ﷻ. This should teach you humility. If the sun and moon and stars humble themselves before their Creator, how much more should you?

وَأَسْبَغَ عَلَيْكُمْ نِعَمَهُۥ ظَـٰهِرَةً وَبَاطِنَةً—and He has completed, He has filled, He has unleashed upon you His favors, both apparent and hidden. The verb أَسْبَغَ means to pour out abundantly, to complete generously, to fill to overflowing. Allah ﷻ has given you blessings you can see—your health, your family, your provisions, your abilities. And He's given you countless blessings you can't even see or recognize—protection from harms you're unaware of, guidance you don't realize you needed, subtle ways He's made your life easier without you noticing.

Here's the connection: when you see the entire universe subjected to Allah ﷻ, you learn humility. When you recognize all the favors He's poured upon you, you become grateful. Humility and gratitude—the two themes from the very beginning—are brought together in this concluding statement.

وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ مَن يُجَـٰدِلُ فِى ٱللَّهِ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ وَلَا هُدًى وَلَا كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ—yet among people there are those who argue about Allah ﷻ without knowledge, without guidance, and without an illuminating book.

The contrast is devastating. Despite all these clear signs, despite the subjugation of the heavens and earth, despite the overwhelming evidence of Allah's ﷻ favors, some people still argue about Him—without any basis. They have no عِلْمٍ (knowledge), no هُدًى (guidance), and no كِتَـٰبٍ مُّنِيرٍ (enlightening scripture).

But now, after this passage, you have all three. You have knowledge of Allah's ﷻ greatness, you have guidance on how to live, and you have this enlightening book—the Quran. The book doesn't just tell you what arguments to make; it shows you how to make them. How to carry yourself, how to walk, how to speak, what humility to show in your facial expressions, what tone to use in your voice.

Because you don't want to be a donkey—knowledgeable in theory but foolish in practice. You want that knowledge to permeate your being, to transform not just what you know but how you live, how you interact, how you present yourself to the world.

This is wisdom. This is what Luqman was given and what he passed on to his son. And this is what Allah ﷻ is now teaching us through this beautiful Surah.

translation: And when it is said to them, "Follow what Allah has revealed," they say, "Rather, we will follow that upon which we found our fathers." Even if Satan was inviting them to the punishment of the Blaze
commentary:
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُمُ ٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ قَالُوا۟ بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ ۚ أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ

When these individuals are told to follow what Allah ﷻ has revealed, their response reveals a fundamental flaw in their reasoning: بَلْ نَتَّبِعُ مَا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَآ (Rather, we will follow what we found our forefathers following). This is the same response given to Prophet Ibrahim (AS) by his own father and people—blind commitment to tradition regardless of its validity.

The contrast being drawn here is striking. The surah just presented the beautiful example of Luqman—a wise father passing down sound guidance rooted in truth. Now it presents the opposite: ancestors passing down baseless traditions that lead to destruction. The implication is clear: not all parental guidance is wise guidance. Not every tradition deserves to be maintained.

Allah ﷻ responds with a powerful rhetorical question: أَوَلَوْ كَانَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَىٰ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (Even if Satan was calling them to the punishment of the blazing Fire?). The word ٱلسَّعِيرِ refers to a fire that roars and crackles—a terrifying, consuming flame.

This rhetorical question exposes the absurdity of blind tradition. Would you really follow your ancestors' ways even if those ways lead directly to Hellfire? On the Day of Judgment, families who followed misguided traditions will stand together—grandfather, father, son, grandson—all pointing fingers at each other. The descendant will say, "We honored you! We had your portrait on our wall! We maintained your ceremonies and rituals! You're the reason we never accepted Islam!" And the ancestor will respond, "Why were you so foolish? I was mistaken, but you had your own mind. It's your own fault."

They'll turn to blame Shaytan, but by then it will be far too late. This passage serves as a warning: examine your family's traditions critically. Just because "this is how we've always done things" doesn't make it right. Just because your parents watched certain content, attended certain events, or practiced certain customs doesn't automatically validate those choices for you.

The ayah about following ancestors appears throughout the Quran, but finds its most extensive treatment in Surah Al-Baqarah. There, Allah ﷻ poses the question: if you want to follow your forefathers, why not follow Ibrahim (AS)? Why do you skip over the best of your ancestors?

translation: And whoever submits his face to Allah while he is a doer of good - then he has grasped the most trustworthy handhold. And to Allah will be the outcome of [all] matters
commentary:
۞ وَمَن يُسْلِمْ وَجْهَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ فَقَدِ ٱسْتَمْسَكَ بِٱلْعُرْوَةِ ٱلْوُثْقَىٰ ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْأُمُورِ

After describing those who reject guidance, Allah ﷻ now presents the opposite: the one who truly submits. The phrase يُسْلِمْ وَجْهَهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ (submits his face to Allah) is deeply significant. Your face represents your entire identity, your direction, your focus. To submit your face to Allah ﷻ means to orient your entire existence toward Him.

But submission alone isn't enough. The ayah adds a critical condition: وَهُوَ مُحْسِنٌ (while being one who excels). The concept of إِحْسَان means to do something beautifully, to perfect it, to give it your absolute best effort. It's not enough to merely go through the motions of worship—you must strive for excellence in your submission to Allah ﷻ.

The reward for this combination of submission and excellence is profound: فَقَدِ ٱسْتَمْسَكَ بِٱلْعُرْوَةِ ٱلْوُثْقَىٰ (such a one has grasped the strongest handhold). This phrase also appears in Surah Al-Baqarah, painting a vivid image. Imagine you've fallen off a ship in a violent storm. The waves are massive, the wind is howling, and you're being pulled under. All you can grab onto is the ship's anchor. Will it hold, or will it snap under the pressure?

Allah ﷻ assures us that the one who submits with excellence has grabbed onto an anchor that will never break, a handhold that will never give way. No matter how fierce the storms of life become, no matter how overwhelming the trials, this handhold—faith combined with righteous action—will never fail you.

The ayah concludes: وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْأُمُورِ (and to Allah is the final return of all matters). Not only will your deeds return to Him, but so will their ultimate judgment and their true value. Everything resolves with Allah ﷻ.

translation: And whoever has disbelieved - let not his disbelief grieve you. To Us is their return, and We will inform them of what they did. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts
commentary:
وَمَن كَفَرَ فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ كُفْرُهُۥٓ ۚ إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ فَنُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ

This ayah provides comfort to the Prophet ﷺ and to every believer who grieves over those who reject faith. وَمَن كَفَرَ فَلَا يَحْزُنكَ كُفْرُهُۥٓ (And whoever disbelieves, let not his disbelief grieve you). The Prophet ﷺ was deeply pained when people rejected Islam, especially those he cared about. Allah ﷻ tells him: don't let their rejection sadden you.

Why shouldn't it grieve us? إِلَيْنَا مَرْجِعُهُمْ (To Us is their return). They will come back to Allah ﷻ, and then فَنُنَبِّئُهُم بِمَا عَمِلُوٓا۟ (We will inform them of what they did). The verb نُنَبِّئُ means to thoroughly inform someone, to give them complete news about something. On the Day of Judgment, every deed will be presented in full detail.

The ayah ends with a powerful reminder: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing of what is in the hearts). The phrase ذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ literally means "the nature of chests"—the inner reality of what lies within hearts. Allah ﷻ knows not just our actions but our intentions, our hidden thoughts, our secret motivations. Nothing is concealed from Him.

translation: We grant them enjoyment for a little; then We will force them to a massive punishment
commentary:
نُمَتِّعُهُمْ قَلِيلًا ثُمَّ نَضْطَرُّهُمْ إِلَىٰ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ

Despite their rejection, Allah ﷻ allows the disbelievers worldly pleasures: نُمَتِّعُهُمْ قَلِيلًا (We give them enjoyment for a little while). They may have health, wealth, success, comfort—all the luxuries this world offers. But this is only قَلِيلًا (a little)—temporary and insignificant in the grand scheme of eternity.

Then comes the inevitable consequence: ثُمَّ نَضْطَرُّهُمْ إِلَىٰ عَذَابٍ غَلِيظٍ (then We will drive them to an unrelenting punishment). The word نَضْطَرُّهُمْ suggests being forced or compelled, driven against one's will. And the punishment is described as غَلِيظٍ—thick, harsh, uncompromising, without any softness or relief.

The word غَلِيظٍ carries the sense of something so solid and dense that it cannot be broken or bent. Imagine concrete that has no cracks, that cannot be penetrated. This describes the nature of the punishment awaiting those who persist in rejection after having received clear guidance.

translation: And if you asked them, "Who created the heavens and earth?" they would surely say, "Allah." Say, "[All] praise is [due] to Allah "; but most of them do not know
commentary:
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ ٱللَّهُ ۚ قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

Allah ﷻ now addresses a profound inconsistency in the beliefs of the disbelievers. If you ask them, "Who created the heavens and the earth?" they would definitely say, "Allah." Even the mushrikoon of Makkah acknowledged Allah ﷻ as the Creator. They didn't dispute His existence or His creative power. Their error lay in associating partners with Him in worship.

Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ: قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (Say: All praise is for Allah). This response is significant. The disbelievers themselves acknowledge the truth of Allah's creation, so the believer's response should be gratitude. Here we see the culmination of Luqman's primary teaching to his son: أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ (Be grateful to Allah).

The concept of ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ encompasses far more than a simple "thank you." It's a comprehensive acknowledgment that all praise, all credit, all gratitude belongs to Allah ﷻ whether we express it or not. It's a statement of objective reality, not merely a command to be obeyed.

Many people misunderstand ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ as a demand from Allah ﷻ to praise Him, as though He needs our praise. They ask, "Why does God want us to praise Him so much? Isn't that egotistical?" But this question reveals a fundamental misunderstanding. ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ isn't God demanding praise—it's a statement of fact. Praise belongs to Allah ﷻ whether any creature offers it or not. Our act of saying Alhamdulillah simply acknowledges this reality and allows us to benefit from that acknowledgment.

The ayah concludes: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (But most of them do not know). They may acknowledge Allah ﷻ as Creator, but they don't truly know what that acknowledgment should mean for their lives. They lack understanding of the implications of their own admission.

translation: To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and earth. Indeed, Allah is the Free of need, the Praiseworthy
commentary:
لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ

Having established Allah's role as Creator, this ayah affirms His ownership: لِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and the earth). Everything in existence is His property, under His dominion, subject to His authority.

Then comes a critical point: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَنِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (Indeed, Allah is the Self-Sufficient, the Praiseworthy). The name ٱلْغَنِىُّ means the One who is completely independent, free from all needs, absolutely self-sufficient and wealthy beyond measure.

Why mention this attribute here? Because the previous ayah discussed calling people to Islam. Someone might think: if these people don't accept Islam, then Allah ﷻ loses something, or something is missing from His dominion. This ayah corrects that misunderstanding. Allah ﷻ is ٱلْغَنِىُّ—completely independent of His creation. Whether people worship Him or reject Him, it adds nothing to Him and takes nothing away from Him.

He is also ٱلْحَمِيدُ—the One deserving of all praise, the One who is inherently praiseworthy. Notice the connection: the previous ayah instructed us to say ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ, and this ayah describes Allah ﷻ as ٱلْحَمِيدُ. Even if no one praises Him, He remains praiseworthy. His praiseworthiness is intrinsic to His nature, not dependent on whether creatures acknowledge it.

This addresses a common misconception. People sometimes ask, "What does God get out of us worshipping Him?" The answer is: nothing. Our worship doesn't benefit Allah ﷻ in any way. He is ٱلْغَنِىُّ—free from all need. Our worship benefits us by aligning us with reality and allowing us to fulfill our purpose.

translation: And if whatever trees upon the earth were pens and the sea [was ink], replenished thereafter by seven [more] seas, the words of Allah would not be exhausted. Indeed, Allah is Exalted in Might and Wise
commentary:
وَلَوْ أَنَّمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلَـٰمٌ وَٱلْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ مَّا نَفِدَتْ كَلِمَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ

This ayah presents one of the most powerful illustrations in the Quran of Allah's infinite nature. وَلَوْ أَنَّمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ مِن شَجَرَةٍ أَقْلَـٰمٌ (If all the trees on earth were pens)—imagine every single tree on the planet transformed into writing instruments. How many pens would one tree yield? Hundreds? Thousands? Now multiply that by every tree on Earth.

وَٱلْبَحْرُ يَمُدُّهُۥ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦ سَبْعَةُ أَبْحُرٍ (And the ocean were ink, supplemented by seven more oceans)—take all the water in all the seas, turn it into ink, and then add seven more oceans' worth of ink to replenish it.

Even with this unimaginable supply of pens and ink, مَّا نَفِدَتْ كَلِمَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ (the Words of Allah would not be exhausted). The "Words of Allah" refers to multiple dimensions: His wisdom, His commands, His decrees, His creative acts (when He says "Be!" and something comes into existence), and the reasons for which He deserves praise.

If you attempted to document every reason to praise Allah ﷻ, every act of His wisdom, every manifestation of His power, every favor He has bestowed—you would run out of ink from eight oceans and pens from every tree on Earth before you finished. His attributes are infinite, His blessings countless, His wisdom boundless.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَزِيزٌ حَكِيمٌ (Indeed, Allah is the Almighty, the All-Wise). He is عَزِيزٌ—ultimate in authority and power, and حَكِيمٌ—perfect in wisdom. These two attributes work in harmony: His power is never exercised without wisdom, and His wisdom is backed by absolute authority.

translation: Your creation and your resurrection will not be but as that of a single soul. Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing
commentary:
مَّا خَلْقُكُمْ وَلَا بَعْثُكُمْ إِلَّا كَنَفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ

After describing something infinite and countless (Allah's Words), the ayah shifts dramatically to something singular and simple: creating and resurrecting humanity. مَّا خَلْقُكُمْ وَلَا بَعْثُكُمْ إِلَّا كَنَفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ (Your creation and your resurrection are only like that of a single soul).

This literary contrast is striking. From pens beyond counting and oceans of ink, we move to: creating all of humanity is no more difficult for Allah ﷻ than creating one person. Resurrecting all of humanity on the Day of Judgment is no more challenging than resurrecting a single individual.

If Allah ﷻ can create one human being, He can create billions. If He can resurrect one, He can resurrect all. The Quran provides examples: Prophet Adam (AS) was created from clay; a man was given death for a hundred years and then brought back to life. If Allah ﷻ can do this for one person, He can do it for all of humanity.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (Indeed, Allah is All-Hearing, All-Seeing). Throughout your life and after your resurrection, Allah ﷻ hears everything you say and sees everything you do. Nothing escapes His awareness.

translation: Do you not see that Allah causes the night to enter the day and causes the day to enter the night and has subjected the sun and the moon, each running [its course] for a specified term, and that Allah, with whatever you do, is Acquainted
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ كُلٌّ يَجْرِىٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

Haven't you observed that Allah ﷻ يُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَيُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ (causes the night to enter into the day and causes the day to enter into the night)? This phrase works on multiple levels. Most obviously, it describes the daily succession of night and day.

But it also refers to the seasonal changes. In certain seasons, nights grow longer and encroach upon the day's territory—the day becomes shorter. Then the pattern reverses, and the day fights back, growing longer while the night shrinks. It's as though night and day are in an eternal competition, each taking turns having the advantage, like armies advancing and retreating across contested territory.

وَسَخَّرَ ٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ (And He has subjected the sun and the moon)—both follow precise schedules set by Allah ﷻ. كُلٌّ يَجْرِىٓ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى (Each running its course for a specified term). The sun governs our daily schedule, rising and setting with perfect regularity. The moon governs our monthly calendar, cycling through its phases.

This has profound implications for worship. The sun determines our five daily prayers—each prayer time is defined by the sun's position. The moon determines our monthly and yearly worship—Ramadan begins with the moon, Hajj is scheduled by the lunar calendar, and our Islamic months follow the moon's cycle.

But there's something deeper here. The ayah ends: وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (And that Allah is All-Aware of what you do). Why mention this after discussing the sun and moon? Because just as these celestial bodies operate on schedules—daily, monthly, seasonally—so too are we being watched constantly, regardless of the time.

The sun and moon become like security cameras in the sky. Whether it's the middle of the night when everyone's asleep, or the middle of the afternoon when everyone's at work and you're home alone, Allah ﷻ sees what you're doing. There is no time when you're not being observed. This is the Quranic equivalent of "24/7"—constant, unceasing awareness through all days and nights, all months and seasons.

translation: That is because Allah is the Truth, and that what they call upon other than Him is falsehood, and because Allah is the Most High, the Grand
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ

All these signs point to one inescapable conclusion: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْحَقُّ (That is because Allah is the Truth). The word ٱلْحَقُّ (Al-Haqq) means the ultimate reality, the absolute truth, that which is real and right in every sense.

وَأَنَّ مَا يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ ٱلْبَـٰطِلُ (And that whatever they call upon besides Him is falsehood). Everything else people turn to—idols, false deities, worldly pursuits given ultimate importance—all of it is بَـٰطِلُ, meaning false, void, empty, without reality or substance.

وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (And that Allah is the Most High, the Most Great). He is elevated above all creation in His attributes, and He is greater than anything the mind can conceive. These divine names—ٱلْعَلِىُّ (the Most High) and ٱلْكَبِيرُ (the Most Great)—remind us that no matter how impressive creation may seem, the Creator is infinitely higher and greater.

translation: Do you not see that ships sail through the sea by the favor of Allah that He may show you of His signs? Indeed in that are signs for everyone patient and grateful
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱلْفُلْكَ تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِنِعْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ لِيُرِيَكُم مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ

Now Allah ﷻ directs our attention to another sign: أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱلْفُلْكَ تَجْرِى فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ بِنِعْمَتِ ٱللَّهِ (Haven't you seen that ships sail through the sea by the grace of Allah?). The same word used for the sun and moon "running" their courses (يَجْرِى) is now used for ships floating in the ocean.

This connection is deliberate. When you think of the sun and moon, you think of stability—they're always there, reliably following their paths. But when you think of a ship at sea, you think of vulnerability. A storm could rise, waves could capsize it, weather could turn deadly. Ships at sea are fragile, dependent entirely on favorable conditions.

By using the same word for both, Allah ﷻ is teaching us something profound: the sun and moon are just as dependent on His mercy as that vulnerable ship. The only reason they continue on their paths is because Allah ﷻ maintains them there. Just as any sailor knows that a ship's safety is in God's hands once it leaves port, so too should we recognize that the entire cosmic order exists only by Allah's continuous mercy.

لِيُرِيَكُم مِّنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦٓ (So He may show you some of His signs). The ships, the seas, the ability to traverse oceans—all are signs of Allah's wisdom and power.

إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (Indeed in that are signs for everyone who is patient and grateful). These two qualities—صَبَّارٍ (extremely patient) and شَكُورٍ (extremely grateful)—are emphasized in their intensive forms. They describe someone who shows patience repeatedly in situation after situation, and who consistently goes out of their way to be grateful.

This echoes the teaching of Luqman earlier in the surah: أَنِ ٱشْكُرْ لِلَّهِ (be grateful to Allah) and وَٱصْبِرْ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَصَابَكَ (be patient over what befalls you). The entire surah has been building toward these twin virtues of patience and gratitude.

translation: And when waves come over them like canopies, they supplicate Allah, sincere to Him in religion. But when He delivers them to the land, there are [some] of them who are moderate [in faith]. And none rejects Our signs except everyone treacherous and ungrateful
commentary:
وَإِذَا غَشِيَهُم مَّوْجٌ كَٱلظُّلَلِ دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ فَمِنْهُم مُّقْتَصِدٌ ۚ وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَّا كُلُّ خَتَّارٍ كَفُورٍ

This ayah describes a dramatic scenario at sea: وَإِذَا غَشِيَهُم مَّوْجٌ كَٱلظُّلَلِ (When waves cover them like shadows). The image is of massive waves, layer upon layer, blocking out the light like dark clouds descending. In Surah Al-Baqarah, Allah ﷻ asks: Are they waiting for the time when He descends from layers of dark clouds? Here, those same layers of darkness come in the form of towering waves.

دَعَوُا۟ ٱللَّهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ ٱلدِّينَ (They call upon Allah, making their faith sincere to Him alone). In that moment of terror, all pretense disappears. No one calls on false gods or worldly solutions. Everyone turns to Allah ﷻ with complete sincerity, their lifestyle (دِّينَ) temporarily becoming purely devoted to Him.

فَلَمَّا نَجَّىٰهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْبَرِّ (But when He saves them to the land), something fascinating happens. Usually, the Quran would say at this point: "Then they return to their shirk." But here, Allah ﷻ describes a different response: فَمِنْهُم مُّقْتَصِدٌ (some of them are moderate).

The word مُقْتَصِدٌ means balanced, middle-ground, moderate. In this context, it means they go back to normal—back to how things were before. Their situation returns to مَا هُوَ عَلَيْهِ (what it always is): nothing special, not extremely good, not extremely bad, just business as usual.

This is Allah's complaint. After a near-death experience, after calling upon Him with complete sincerity, after being miraculously saved—how do you just move on and forget? How do you return to your previous state as though nothing happened? Something should have changed. You shouldn't be the same person you were before. The experience should have shifted your priorities, pulled you closer to Allah ﷻ, left lasting effects.

But many people treat such experiences like just another episode in their lives, with no residual impact.

وَمَا يَجْحَدُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ إِلَّا كُلُّ خَتَّارٍ كَفُورٍ (And none reject Our signs except the one who is extremely treacherous and ungrateful). The word خَتَّارٍ refers to someone who betrays in the worst way—a backstabber, a traitor, someone who cons others without mercy. It's similar to غَدَّارٍ (ghaddar), one who abandons his allies.

And كَفُورٍ (kafoor) means extremely, excessively ungrateful—the opposite of شَكُورٍ (shakoor), extremely grateful, mentioned in the previous ayah.

The surah has built this comparison carefully: on one side, you have patience (صَبَّارٍ) versus treachery (خَتَّارٍ). On the other side, you have extreme gratitude (شَكُورٍ) versus extreme ingratitude (كَفُورٍ). The contrast between gratitude and ingratitude is obvious. But what's the connection between patience and treachery?

The patient person remains loyal to what they learned. They don't forget the lessons from their trials. They hold onto their faith through difficulties. The treacherous person, however, betrays what they learned. They experience a profound moment of clarity, call upon Allah ﷻ sincerely, get saved—and then abandon all of it, betraying the very covenant they made in that moment of need.

translation: O mankind, fear your Lord and fear a Day when no father will avail his son, nor will a son avail his father at all. Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth, so let not the worldly life delude you and be not deceived about Allah by the Deceiver
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ وَٱخْشَوْا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا يَجْزِى وَالِدٌ عَن وَلَدِهِۦ وَلَا مَوْلُودٌ هُوَ جَازٍ عَن وَالِدِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا ۚ إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ ۖ فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ

The surah now comes full circle with a powerful address to all humanity: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ٱتَّقُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ (O humanity! Be mindful of your Lord). Two commands follow: be mindful of Allah ﷻ and fear a specific Day.

وَٱخْشَوْا۟ يَوْمًا لَّا يَجْزِى وَالِدٌ عَن وَلَدِهِۦ (And fear a Day when no father can avail his child in any way). This is remarkable. The surah began with a beautiful portrait of fatherly love—Luqman advising his son with wisdom, compassion, and concern. Now, at the end, we're told that even that loving relationship will be powerless on the Day of Judgment.

وَلَا مَوْلُودٌ هُوَ جَازٍ عَن وَالِدِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا (Nor will a child avail his father in any way). Neither can rescue the other. Neither can pay the other's debt or compensate for the other's sins.

إِنَّ وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ حَقٌّ (Indeed, the promise of Allah is truth). What promise? The promise of that Day, the promise of judgment, the promise of accountability.

فَلَا تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا (So let not worldly life deceive you). The word تَغُرَّنَّكُمُ comes from غُرُور, meaning to deceive someone by taking advantage of their heedlessness, their lack of awareness. Worldly life—with its glitter, its pleasures, its temporary satisfactions—can trick us into forgetting what truly matters.

What did the surah highlight at its beginning as the most tempting aspect of worldly life? لَهْوُ ٱلْحَدِيثِ (idle entertainment). Don't think that life is only about entertainment, only about amusement, only about passing time pleasantly.

Consider young people today who live from one entertainment release to the next—waiting for the next season of a show, the next video game installment, the next movie premiere. Their lives revolve around these temporary pleasures. They know every detail about fictional characters but remain ignorant about their Creator. They follow the lives of celebrities but don't follow the guidance of prophets.

Some youth actually feel upset when Ramadan coincides with the release of a highly anticipated movie. They're torn between fasting and attending opening night. This reveals how deeply entertainment has enslaved hearts that should be devoted to Allah ﷻ.

The ayah continues: وَلَا يَغُرَّنَّكُم بِٱللَّهِ ٱلْغَرُورُ (And let not the Deceiver deceive you concerning Allah). ٱلْغَرُورُ (Al-Gharoor) is a name of Shaytan—the ultimate deceiver who takes advantage of human heedlessness. He never rests, even when we're at rest. He's constantly working to make us forget Allah ﷻ.

Here's the profound difference between what Allah ﷻ offers the heart and what entertainment offers. People stand in line for hours to see a movie, seeking rest for their restless hearts. They come out saying, "It was alright, I guess." When you ask if they want to see it again, the answer is lukewarm at best. It was empty, unsatisfying. They need the sequel to feel complete.

But people who stand in prayer before Allah ﷻ, who recite and listen to His words—they want to return again and again. "Let's pray Taraweeh tonight. Let's listen to Quran again." Why? Because it actually feeds the heart. It genuinely provides peace. It doesn't leave you thirstier. This is the difference between the deceptive entertainment of worldly life and the true satisfaction found in connection with Allah ﷻ.

translation: Indeed, Allah [alone] has knowledge of the Hour and sends down the rain and knows what is in the wombs. And no soul perceives what it will earn tomorrow, and no soul perceives in what land it will die. Indeed, Allah is Knowing and Acquainted
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ وَيُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ ۖ وَمَا تَدْرِى نَفْسٌ مَّاذَا تَكْسِبُ غَدًا ۖ وَمَا تَدْرِى نَفْسٌۢ بِأَىِّ أَرْضٍ تَمُوتُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌۢ

The surah concludes with an ayah that should instill in us a profound sense of urgency: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥ عِلْمُ ٱلسَّاعَةِ (Indeed, Allah alone has knowledge of the Hour). Why is this mentioned last? Because an hour creates more urgency than a day. In old Arabic, ٱلسَّاعَةِ doesn't just mean "the Hour"—it can mean "the Moment." The Final Moment could arrive at any time, and Allah ﷻ alone knows when.

We have no right to say, "We have time. The signs of the Hour haven't all been fulfilled yet. The Dajjal hasn't come. Yajuj and Majuj haven't emerged. We're safe for now." Allah ﷻ is telling us: you don't know. I alone know the timing.

وَيُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ (And He sends down the rain). Rain gives life. Allah ﷻ continues to send life-giving rain to the earth until the Final Hour arrives. Just as rain in the right amount gives life to dead earth, allowing it to become "pregnant" with vegetation and "give birth" to plants, so too does Allah ﷻ sustain all life until its appointed end.

وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ (And He knows what is in the wombs). Allah ﷻ speaks of the ultimate death (the Hour), then mentions how life continues (rain and earth), then mentions how human life begins (in the womb). Only He knows what each womb carries.

وَمَا تَدْرِى نَفْسٌ مَّاذَا تَكْسِبُ غَدًا (And no soul knows what it will earn tomorrow). This is critical. We don't know what's coming. One phone call can change everything. One letter in the mail can alter your entire life. One accident can end it all.

When someone hears this—that no person knows what they'll be able to earn tomorrow—what is Allah ﷻ really saying? Earn now. Act now. Don't delay. You don't know if tomorrow will come for you.

وَمَا تَدْرِى نَفْسٌۢ بِأَىِّ أَرْضٍ تَمُوتُ (And no soul knows in what land it will die). The word أَرْضٍ here doesn't mean country or region—it means the specific ground beneath your feet. You don't know if you'll die at home, at work, in transit, at the hospital, or somewhere completely unexpected.

I know of a young man, 33 years old, healthy, with a wife and children. One Sunday morning, he woke up, made breakfast for his kids, and collapsed. He died right there, suddenly, from a heart attack. No warning. No prior symptoms. No genetic predisposition. The cause? Heart attack. The trigger? Unknown.

You are standing right now on the earth where you might die. You just don't know it.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌ خَبِيرٌۢ (Indeed, Allah is All-Knowing, All-Aware). He knows where you stand and when your time will come. He has complete knowledge and complete awareness.

This final ayah creates urgency about everything the surah taught. Parents must give wise counsel now—you don't know how much time you have. Children must honor parents now—you don't know when they'll be gone. Everyone must be grateful to Allah ﷻ now, be patient now, hold fast to faith now.

The surah opened with wisdom—the wisdom of gratitude. It explored patient parenting, wise counsel, rejection of baseless tradition, and sincere submission to Allah ﷻ. And it closes by reminding us: act on this wisdom immediately. Time is not guaranteed.

May Allah ﷻ make us among those who understand the wisdom of these ayahs, who show patience in adversity, and who remain constantly grateful to Him. May He protect us from the deceptions of this worldly life and from the treachery of Shaytan. And may He grant us the urgency to live every moment in consciousness of our return to Him.
